attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the birth suite with a two-hour history of clear fluid loss from her vagina at 32 weeks gestation. She is not experiencing any contractions. This is her first pregnancy. Her antenatal care has been uncomplicated, except for a positive Group B streptococcus (GBS) urine culture at her booking visit. She has not had any vaginal swabs since then. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. On abdominal examination, the uterus is soft and non-tender, with a fundal height consistent with dates. The fetal heart rate is 140 beats per minute. Sterile speculum examination confirms the presence of clear fluid in the vagina, with a positive actim PROM test.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Neonatal steroids and immediate induction of labour
Neonatal steroids and maternal antibiotics followed by induction of labour in 48 hours
Neonatal steroids, maternal antibiotics and conservative management until 37 weeks
Neonatal steroids, maternal antibiotics and conservative management until 34 weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) at 32 weeks gestation, complicated by Group B streptococcus (GBS) colonisation. The most appropriate initial management is neonatal steroids, maternal antibiotics and conservative management until 37 weeks. Management of PPROM differs from term ruptured membranes (37 weeks+) in the setting of positive GBS where immediate induction of labour would be recommended.

Neonatal steroids (corticosteroids) are indicated in PPROM before 34 weeks gestation to promote fetal lung maturity and reduce the risk of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular haemorrhage, and necrotising enterocolitis. A course of betamethasone typically consists of two 12 mg doses given 24 hours apart.

Maternal antibiotics are crucial in this case. Intravenous benzylpenicillin is the first-line antibiotic for GBS prophylaxis. This patient's positive GBS urine culture at booking indicates heavy colonisation, which is an indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regardless of subsequent swab results. Benzylpenicillin should be started immediately and IV antibiotics should also be given in labour.

Conservative management until 37 weeks is appropriate in the absence of complications even with GBS as the risks of prematurity outweigh the risks of infection. After initiating corticosteroids and antibiotics, the patient should be admitted for monitoring of maternal and fetal wellbeing. The timing of delivery will depend on various factors, including gestational age, signs of infection or labour, and fetal status. If there are signs of developing chorioamnionitis then this plan is reassessed and delivery occurs earlier but aim is to reach 37 weeks if safe to do so.

Neonatal steroids and immediate induction of labour is not appropriate as there is no indication for immediate delivery. PPROM at 32 weeks without signs of infection or fetal distress can be managed conservatively.

Immediate induction of labour alone is not recommended as it does not allow time for the beneficial effects of corticosteroids on fetal lung maturity.

Neonatal steroids and maternal antibiotics followed by induction of labour in 48 hours is too early for delivery. In the absence of complications, expectant management beyond 48 hours is preferred to allow further fetal maturation.

Neonatal steroids, maternal antibiotics and conservative management until 34 weeks is a reasonable approach, but continuing conservative management until 37 weeks (if no complications arise) provides the best outcomes for the neonate.

In summary, the management of PPROM involves a balance between reducing the risks of prematurity and managing the risks of prolonged rupture of membranes in the setting of GBS. Corticosteroids and antibiotics are key interventions in PPROM, with the timing of delivery determined by individual clinical factors.
learningPoints: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) requires prompt administration of antibiotics to reduce the risk of neonatal sepsis.
Antenatal corticosteroids are indicated in PPROM before 34 weeks gestation to promote fetal lung maturity.
Group B streptococcus (GBS) in urine during pregnancy indicates heavy colonisation and requires intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis however in PPROM the risks of prematurity outweigh the risk of GBS.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases (ASID). Management of perinatal infections. Melbourne: The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG); 2014. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Prevention of early-onset group B streptococcal disease. Green-top Guideline No. 36. London: RCOG; 2017. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/prevention-of-early-onset-group-b-streptococcal-disease-green-top-guideline-no-36/
